facts.text: UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage program helps protect living traditions such as festivals, crafts, music, and rituals.
Lunar New Year is celebrated across many parts of East and Southeast Asia, with traditions centered on family, food, and renewal.
Diwali is widely known as the Festival of Lights and is celebrated by millions of people across India and the global Indian diaspora.
Holi in India is famous for joyful gatherings and colorful powder thrown during the celebration of spring.
Ramadan is observed by Muslims worldwide as a month of fasting, reflection, prayer, and community.
Eid al-Fitr marks the end of Ramadan and is celebrated with prayers, family visits, and festive meals.
Día de los Muertos, or Day of the Dead, honors loved ones who have died and is one of Mexico's most recognized cultural traditions.
Nowruz, the Persian New Year, is celebrated around the spring equinox in Iran and many neighboring cultures.
Oktoberfest began in Munich in 1810 as a royal wedding celebration and grew into the world's most famous beer festival.
Rio de Janeiro's Carnival is one of the largest and most famous cultural festivals in the world.
Hanami in Japan centers on appreciating the beauty of cherry blossoms each spring.
The Japanese tea ceremony, often called chanoyu, emphasizes ritual, hospitality, and aesthetics.
Sauna culture is so central to Finland that the country has more saunas than cars.
Flamenco from Spain combines singing, guitar, dance, and rhythmic handclaps and is recognized by UNESCO.
Tango developed in the Río de la Plata region of Argentina and Uruguay and became one of the world's most famous partner dances.
Capoeira from Brazil blends martial arts, dance, music, and acrobatics into one cultural tradition.
Mariachi music is one of the best-known cultural traditions of Mexico and was added to UNESCO's heritage list.
Fado is a Portuguese music tradition known for expressive singing and themes of longing and fate.
Kintsugi in Japan is the art of repairing broken pottery with lacquer mixed with precious metal, embracing visible repair.
Songkran in Thailand marks the traditional New Year and is famous for public water-splashing celebrations.
The haka is a traditional Māori ceremonial dance and chant from Aotearoa New Zealand.
Midsummer celebrations in Scandinavia revolve around long daylight hours, bonfires, flowers, and community gatherings.
The Highland games in Scotland feature traditional sports, piping, dancing, and clan heritage.
Venice Carnival became famous for its elaborate masks and costumes.
San Fermín in Pamplona is globally known for the running of the bulls, but it also includes music, parades, and religious traditions.
Naadam in Mongolia celebrates wrestling, horse racing, and archery as the country's traditional 'Three Manly Games.'
Inti Raymi in Peru honors the Inca sun god and remains a major cultural celebration in Cusco.
Bastille Day on July 14 is France's national day and commemorates key events of the French Revolution.
Kwanzaa was created in 1966 as a celebration of African American culture and community.
The Chinese zodiac follows a repeating 12-year cycle in which each year is represented by an animal.
Lunar New Year is celebrated across many parts of East and Southeast Asia with family rituals, food, and renewal themes.
Its many regional traditions make it one of the most widely recognized seasonal celebrations in the world.
Lunar New Year is one of the standout traditions or topics associated with World Culture.
Readers and travelers interested in World Culture often encounter Lunar New Year very quickly.
Learning about Lunar New Year helps explain part of the appeal and diversity of World Culture.
For many people around the world, Lunar New Year is a memorable part of World Culture.
Documentaries, articles, and travel guides about World Culture frequently highlight Lunar New Year.
The story of Lunar New Year connects directly to the broader traditions behind World Culture.
Lunar New Year is frequently used to introduce newcomers to the richness of World Culture.

One reason World Culture feels so vivid and memorable is the presence of traditions and foods such as Lunar New Year.
From heritage to everyday life, Lunar New Year plays a notable role in how World Culture is experienced and described.
